=== Frontmatter ===
---
name: post-implementation-audit
description: MANDATORY after every implementation task — runs three sequential audits (spec drift, iterative "anything missing?", test coverage) on the new code and lands every finding in the same PR before the user-review step of the CLAUDE.md workflow. Trigger whenever an implementation task has just had its named tests pass and you are about to present the result to the user. Also trigger on phrases like "ready to present", "implementation is complete", "named tests pass", "before opening the PR", or "ready for review". Skipping this skill leaves spec drift, untested defensive code, missing conventions, and silent contract violations in the merged PR — bugs that took the user three round-trips to catch on task #204 will all merge silently if this audit is not run.
---

# Post-implementation audit

This skill is the bridge between *"the named tests pass"* and *"the user reviews the diff"*. It exists because the initial implementation of any task is usually correct on its surface acceptance criteria but quietly drifts from the spec, leaves untested defensive code, ships APIs that diverge from convention, or carries a fold-vs-lowercase mistake that won't surface until production. Running these audits before user-review collapses three reviewer round-trips into one.

The skill is mandatory per CLAUDE.md "Development Workflow" step 7 — run it after implementing any user-requested task (feature, bug fix, refactor) and before presenting the final result for user review.

=== Section headings ===
12:## Operating principle: no deferral
20:## Three sequential phases
26:### Phase 1 — spec/implementation drift audit
61:### Phase 2 — iterative "anything else missing?" passes
96:### Phase 3 — test coverage audit
160:## After the three phases
196:## Anti-patterns
206:## What this skill is not
212:## Worked example — task #204

content.text:
Skill is well-structured, 221 lines (under the 500-line target), all the canonical entry-prompts you used on task #204 are embedded verbatim, and the worked example references this very task as the validation case.

## What landed

| File | Change | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **`.claude/skills/post-implementation-audit/SKILL.md`** (new, 221 lines) | The skill itself, with three phases, the verbatim entry-prompts you used, the canonical termination rules, the verification suite, anti-patterns, and the task #204 worked example as proof | Codifies the workflow you and I refined over the last six rounds on this task |
| **`AGENTS.md`** (CLAUDE.md symlinks here) — new step 7 in "Development Workflow" | Marks the skill as **mandatory** after implementation; steps 8–12 renumbered; "Skipping this step is a contract violation" language matches the spirit of your "no deferral, same PR" rule | Forces every future implementation task to run the skill before user-review |

## Anatomy of the skill

```
post-implementation-audit/
└── SKILL.md
    ├── frontmatter: pushy description with multiple trigger phrases + reference to task #204
    ├── intro: why this skill exists (3-round-trip collapse)
    ├── Operating principle: no deferral
    ├── Phase 1 — spec/impl drift
    │   ├── canonical entry-prompt (your exact words)
    │   ├── 4-step procedure
    │   ├── output table format
    │   └── "land everything" rule
    ├── Phase 2 — iterative "anything else?"
    │   ├── canonical entry-prompt (your exact words)
    │   ├── 7-item surface checklist (adapter specs, feature wiring, prelude, manifest, foundation, deps, mutants.toml)
    │   ├── termination rule (≥2 clean rounds, not 1)
    │   └── output format per round
    ├── Phase 3 — test coverage
    │   ├── canonical entry-prompt (your exact words)
    │   ├── 8-row test-type table (unit, snapshot, proptest, mutation, doc, spec-conformance, llvm-cov, e2e)
    │   ├── 4-step procedure (coverage → mutants → breadth → cumulative)
    │   └── anti-pattern: chasing 100% line coverage
    ├── Verification suite (the 8 commands run after every phase)
    ├── Anti-patterns (deferral, option-loading, single-round, coverage-as-goal)
    ├── What this skill is *not* (clear delineation vs code-review, planning, acceptance-criteria revisitation)
    └── Worked example: task #204 with concrete numbers
```

## How it triggers

The skill is now in the available-skills list and triggers automatically via:

1. **CLAUDE.md step 7** — the workflow itself mandates invoking the skill.
2. **Description phrases** — "ready to present", "implementation is complete", "named tests pass", "before opening the PR", "ready for review".
3. **Task #204 reference in the description** — embeds the proof case so the trigger model has a concrete success example.

## What this changes for future work

Previously: implementation → present → user surfaces drift → fix → present → user surfaces tests → fix → present (3 rounds).

Now: implementation → **post-implementation-audit (3 phases, all findings landed)** → present (1 round).

The skill is now ready. Want me to proceed with `cargo xci` + commit + PR for the underlying task #204 work, with the skill + CLAUDE.md update included in the same commit (since they're part of the same "what we learned on this task" delivery)?

attachment.content:
=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

===================================

input.description: Check xtask coverage commands and CLAUDE.md guidance

content.text: You're right — bare `cargo llvm-cov` skips wasm-only paths in adapter crates. The skill needs to encode the workspace-specific routing. Updating Phase 3.

input.old_string:
#### Procedure

1. **Coverage report.** Run:
   ```bash
   cargo llvm-cov test -p <crate> --features <relevant> --lib --no-fail-fast --text -- <module>::
   ```
   Read the per-line annotated output. Every `^0` marker is an unhit branch — investigate each one. Categorize as: real test gap / equivalent defensive guard / unreachable dead code.
input.new_string:
#### Procedure

1. **Coverage report.** The command depends on which crate the change touches — this workspace measures host-target and wasm32 paths separately because adapter code (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, the `ars-i18n` `web-intl` path, the adapter test harnesses) has `target_arch = "wasm32"` / `feature = "web"` code that bare host-target `cargo llvm-cov` cannot reach.

   **For native-only crates** (`ars-components`, `ars-collections`, `ars-core`, `ars-forms`, `ars-interactions`, `ars-a11y`, `xtask`):
   ```bash
   cargo llvm-cov test -p <crate> --features <relevant> --lib --no-fail-fast --text -- <module>::
   ```

   **For adapter crates / `web-intl` paths** (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, `ars-test-harness-leptos`, `ars-test-harness-dioxus`, or `ars-i18n` when the change is in the `web-intl` feature gate):
   ```bash
   cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <crate> --features <relevant> --output <crate>.lcov.info
   ```
   This uses `wasm-bindgen-test`'s experimental coverage recipe with LLVM 22 / `clang-22` (the same recipe CI nightly runs via `cargo xtask ci coverage`). The bare `cargo llvm-cov` command will silently report `0.00%` for the wasm-gated code paths, which is *not* the same as having no coverage — it means *unmeasured*. Don't trust low numbers on adapter crates from the native path.

   **For a change that touches both native and wasm paths** (e.g., a new ars-i18n helper consumed from both backends, or a refactor in `ars-dom` that affects native callers):
   ```bash
   # Generate both:
   cargo llvm-cov --workspace --exclude ars-leptos --exclude ars-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-test-harness-leptos --exclude ars-test-harness-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-derive --exclude xtask \
     --lcov --output-path native.lcov.info

   cargo xtask coverage merge --inputs native.lcov.info wasm.lcov.info --output merged.lcov.info
   # Enforce per-crate spec-defined thresholds:
   cargo xtask coverage check-all --file merged.lcov.info
   ```

   The per-crate thresholds (line% / branch%) are encoded in `xtask::coverage::default_thresholds()` and include the wasm targets. `cargo xtask coverage check-all` is the same gate CI enforces, so a green local run guarantees green CI on the coverage step.

   Read the per-line annotated output from the `--text` invocations. Every `^0` marker is an unhit branch — investigate each one. Categorize as: real test gap / equivalent defensive guard / unreachable dead code. **Expected non-gaps:** no-op callback closures in tests (e.g., `|_| {}`) are intentionally uncovered and not a real gap — CLAUDE.md calls this out explicitly.

#### Procedure

1. **Coverage report.** The command depends on which crate the change touches — this workspace measures host-target and wasm32 paths separately because adapter code (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, the `ars-i18n` `web-intl` path, the adapter test harnesses) has `target_arch = "wasm32"` / `feature = "web"` code that bare host-target `cargo llvm-cov` cannot reach.

   **For native-only crates** (`ars-components`, `ars-collections`, `ars-core`, `ars-forms`, `ars-interactions`, `ars-a11y`, `xtask`):
   ```bash
   cargo llvm-cov test -p <crate> --features <relevant> --lib --no-fail-fast --text -- <module>::
   ```

   **For adapter crates / `web-intl` paths** (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, `ars-test-harness-leptos`, `ars-test-harness-dioxus`, or `ars-i18n` when the change is in the `web-intl` feature gate):
   ```bash
   cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <crate> --features <relevant> --output <crate>.lcov.info
   ```
   This uses `wasm-bindgen-test`'s experimental coverage recipe with LLVM 22 / `clang-22` (the same recipe CI nightly runs via `cargo xtask ci coverage`). The bare `cargo llvm-cov` command will silently report `0.00%` for the wasm-gated code paths, which is *not* the same as having no coverage — it means *unmeasured*. Don't trust low numbers on adapter crates from the native path.

   **For a change that touches both native and wasm paths** (e.g., a new ars-i18n helper consumed from both backends, or a refactor in `ars-dom` that affects native callers):
   ```bash
   # Generate both:
   cargo llvm-cov --workspace --exclude ars-leptos --exclude ars-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-test-harness-leptos --exclude ars-test-harness-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-derive --exclude xtask \
     --lcov --output-path native.lcov.info
   cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <wasm-package> --output wasm.lcov.info
   # Merge without double-counting duplicate lines:
   cargo xtask coverage merge --inputs native.lcov.info wasm.lcov.info --output merged.lcov.info
   # Enforce per-crate spec-defined thresholds:
   cargo xtask coverage check-all --file merged.lcov.info
   ```

   The per-crate thresholds (line% / branch%) are encoded in `xtask::coverage::default_thresholds()` and include the wasm targets. `cargo xtask coverage check-all` is the same gate CI enforces, so a green local run guarantees green CI on the coverage step.

   **Not measured by either path:** `ars-derive` (proc-macro, runs in the compiler — exercised indirectly via `crates/ars-core/tests/derive_contract.rs`). Don't expect coverage numbers here.

#### Anti-pattern: chasing 100% line coverage

The goal is **not** "100% line coverage at any cost". The goal is *"no surviving mutations, no unreachable defensive code, every test type that should exist does exist"*. A 99% line-coverage report with all mutations caught is a stronger position than a 100% line-coverage report with three surviving mutations. If the last 1% of uncovered code is a genuinely unreachable defensive guard, document it in `mutants.toml` and move on.

---

## After the three phases

Run the full verification suite one final time before handing off to the user:

```bash
# 1. Unit + integration tests, both feature-on and feature-off variants
cargo test -p <crate> --features <relevant> --all-targets

# 2. Workspace clippy (must be -D warnings clean)

cargo clippy -p ars-i18n --no-default-features --features std,web-intl --all-targets -- -D warnings

221	Then write **one consolidated user summary** covering all three phases' findings + the verification table. Hand off for user review per CLAUDE.md workflow step 8 (which was the old step 7 before this skill was inserted).
222	
223	## Anti-patterns
224	
225	- **Deferring** any finding ("we can fix that in a follow-up"). This is the rule the user set explicitly. Breaking it makes the audit ceremonial.
226	- **Loading the user with options mid-phase.** Make a clear best-outcome recommendation. Only escalate to `AskUserQuestion` when there's a genuine judgment call the user must make (e.g., behavior choices with real tradeoffs, not implementation details).
227	- **Skipping rounds of Phase 2** because the first round was clean. The second round nearly always finds something the first ignored.
228	- **Treating coverage % as the goal.** The goal is mutation-killed and breadth-complete, not a number.
229	- **Inventing cosmetic edits** the audits didn't surface. If a phase doesn't find a real finding, don't fabricate one — say "this round found nothing" and proceed.
230	- **Re-running tests after every micro-edit** during a phase. Land a batch of related findings, then verify, then move on.
231	- **Forgetting to update the named-tests file** when a new test exposes a real bug fixed in the same phase. The fix and the test land together.
232	
233	## What this skill is not
234	
235	- A code-review skill. Code review (style, naming, simplification) belongs to `pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer` and runs after the PR is opened.
236	- A pre-implementation skill. Before implementing, `feature-dev:feature-dev` and `superpowers:writing-plans` handle planning.
237	- A way to revisit acceptance criteria. The acceptance criteria are fixed by the GitHub issue. This skill assumes they're already met.

```

Then write **one consolidated user summary** covering all three phases' findings + the verification table. Hand off for user review per CLAUDE.md workflow step 8 (which was the old step 7 before this skill was inserted).

## Anti-patterns

- **Deferring** any finding ("we can fix that in a follow-up"). This is the rule the user set explicitly. Breaking it makes the audit ceremonial.
- **Loading the user with options mid-phase.** Make a clear best-outcome recommendation. Only escalate to `AskUserQuestion` when there's a genuine judgment call the user must make (e.g., behavior choices with real tradeoffs, not implementation details).
- **Skipping rounds of Phase 2** because the first round was clean. The second round nearly always finds something the first ignored.
- **Treating coverage % as the goal.** The goal is mutation-killed and breadth-complete, not a number.
- **Inventing cosmetic edits** the audits didn't surface. If a phase doesn't find a real finding, don't fabricate one — say "this round found nothing" and proceed.
- **Re-running tests after every micro-edit** during a phase. Land a batch of related findings, then verify, then move on.
- **Forgetting to update the named-tests file** when a new test exposes a real bug fixed in the same phase. The fix and the test land together.

## What this skill is not

- A code-review skill. Code review (style, naming, simplification) belongs to `pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer` and runs after the PR is opened.
- A pre-implementation skill. Before implementing, `feature-dev:feature-dev` and `superpowers:writing-plans` handle planning.
- A way to revisit acceptance criteria. The acceptance criteria are fixed by the GitHub issue. This skill assumes they're already met.

content.text: My skill had the wrong flag names — actual API uses `--file` and `--feature` (singular, repeatable), not `--output`/`--features`. Fixing.

input.old_string:
**For adapter crates / `web-intl` paths** (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, `ars-test-harness-leptos`, `ars-test-harness-dioxus`, or `ars-i18n` when the change is in the `web-intl` feature gate):
   ```bash
   cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <crate> --features <relevant> --output <crate>.lcov.info
   ```
   This uses `wasm-bindgen-test`'s experimental coverage recipe with LLVM 22 / `clang-22` (the same recipe CI nightly runs via `cargo xtask ci coverage`). The bare `cargo llvm-cov` command will silently report `0.00%` for the wasm-gated code paths, which is *not* the same as having no coverage — it means *unmeasured*. Don't trust low numbers on adapter crates from the native path.

   **For a change that touches both native and wasm paths** (e.g., a new ars-i18n helper consumed from both backends, or a refactor in `ars-dom` that affects native callers):
   ```bash
   # Generate both:
   cargo llvm-cov --workspace --exclude ars-leptos --exclude ars-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-test-harness-leptos --exclude ars-test-harness-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-derive --exclude xtask \
     --lcov --output-path native.lcov.info
   cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <wasm-package> --output wasm.lcov.info
   # Merge without double-counting duplicate lines:
   cargo xtask coverage merge --inputs native.lcov.info wasm.lcov.info --output merged.lcov.info
   # Enforce per-crate spec-defined thresholds:
   cargo xtask coverage check-all --file merged.lcov.info
   ```
input.new_string:
**For adapter crates / `web-intl` paths** (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, `ars-test-harness-leptos`, `ars-test-harness-dioxus`, or `ars-i18n` when the change is in the `web-intl` feature gate):
   ```bash
   cargo xtask coverage wasm \
     --package <crate> \
     --feature <feat1> --feature <feat2> \
     --file wasm.lcov.info
   ```
   (`--feature` is singular and repeatable, NOT `--features` — it mirrors cargo's `--feature` convention; use `--no-default-features` to drop defaults.) This uses `wasm-bindgen-test`'s experimental coverage recipe with LLVM 22 / `clang-22` (the same recipe CI nightly runs via `cargo xtask ci coverage`). The bare `cargo llvm-cov` command will silently report `0.00%` for the wasm-gated code paths, which is *not* the same as having no coverage — it means *unmeasured*. Don't trust low numbers on adapter crates from the native path.

   **For a change that touches both native and wasm paths** (e.g., a new ars-i18n helper consumed from both backends, or a refactor in `ars-dom` that affects native callers):
   ```bash
   # Generate the native lcov (workspace, excluding wasm-only crates):
   cargo llvm-cov --workspace \
     --exclude ars-leptos --exclude ars-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-test-harness-leptos --exclude ars-test-harness-dioxus \
     --exclude ars-derive --exclude xtask \
     --lcov --output-path native.lcov.info

   # Generate the wasm lcov for each adapter target you touched:
   cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <wasm-package> --feature <feat> --file wasm.lcov.info

   # Merge without double-counting duplicate lines (each `--file` adds an input):
   cargo xtask coverage merge \
     --file native.lcov.info \
     --file wasm.lcov.info \
     --output merged.lcov.info

   # Enforce per-crate spec-defined thresholds (same gate CI runs):
   cargo xtask coverage check-all --file merged.lcov.info
   ```

content.text:
Skill is now 266 lines (still well under the 500-line target). The Phase 3 coverage section now correctly routes by crate:

| Crate touched | Coverage command |
|---|---|
| **Native-only** (`ars-components`, `ars-collections`, `ars-core`, `ars-forms`, `ars-interactions`, `ars-a11y`, `xtask`) | `cargo llvm-cov test -p <crate> --features ... --text -- <module>::` |
| **Adapter / wasm** (`ars-leptos`, `ars-dioxus`, `ars-dom`, adapter test harnesses, `ars-i18n` web-intl path) | `cargo xtask coverage wasm --package <crate> --feature <f> --file out.lcov.info` |
| **Both** (rare: ars-i18n helpers consumed cross-backend, ars-dom refactors with native callers) | Generate both, then `cargo xtask coverage merge --file native --file wasm --output merged.lcov.info` |
| **Threshold gate** (same one CI runs) | `cargo xtask coverage check-all --file <lcov>` |

I also fixed:
- **Wrong flag names** (was `--features <list>` and `--output`; actual API is `--feature <name>` repeated and `--file`) — verified against `cargo xtask coverage wasm --help`.
- **Misleading "0% means no coverage"** — added the explicit warning that bare `cargo llvm-cov` reports `0.00%` for wasm-gated paths because they're *unmeasured*, not actually uncovered. Easy to misread as a real gap and chase phantom tests.
- **E2E row** in the test-types table now explicitly says wasm-bindgen-test, references `cargo xtask test`, and tells you to trust the wasm-path coverage number not the host-target one.
- **Verification suite step 8** now references `cargo xtask coverage check-all` (the actual CI gate) instead of implying bare `cargo llvm-cov` is sufficient.
- **`ars-derive`** is now flagged as unmeasured by either path (proc-macro, runs in the compiler).

Good catch — under the old wording, a future audit of an `ars-leptos` change would have produced false-positive coverage gaps (host-target reports 0% for csr-only code) and missed real ones (the wasm path wasn't run at all).

=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |

content.text:
# My take on the six findings

Honest assessment: **4 of 6 are worth landing, 2 of 6 are the linter applying generic templates that don't fit a workflow skill of this kind.** Let me go through each.

---

### Finding 1 — "trigger phrases are ambiguous" (severity 4) → ✅ minor fix worth landing

**Linter's take:** "It is unclear whether all these phrases must be present, or if any one of them suffices."

**My take:** Half-valid. The current wording "*Also trigger on phrases like X, Y, Z*" is interpreted correctly by any model with normal English comprehension — "or" lists are disjunctive. But the linter has a point that explicit > implicit. Adding "any of" costs nothing and removes a potential misreading.

**Action:** Land — change `"Also trigger on phrases like X, Y, Z"` → `"Also trigger when any of these phrases appear: X, Y, Z"`.

---

### Finding 2 — "constraint overload in description" (severity 4) → ✅ partial fix worth landing

**Linter's take:** "Numerous constraints... may overwhelm the model... break into smaller clearly prioritized steps."

**My take:** Partly valid, partly category-error.
- **Valid:** The description is 124 words; framework recommends ~100. Tightening helps.
- **Category error:** The three constraints the linter flags (*no deferral*, *same PR*, *three sequential audits*) are not overlapping — they're the **three distinct things this skill does**. Compressing them out of the description doesn't help triggering; it makes the description ambiguous about what the skill is for. And "break into smaller prioritized steps" is exactly what the **body** does — `## Operating principle: no deferral` is its own section, and the three phases each have their own H3.

The linter is applying a generic "long-instruction = bad" template, but this is a workflow skill whose description has to convey three orthogonal claims (when, what, why-it-matters) to a model deciding whether to invoke it.

**Action:** Tighten the description from 124 → ~95 words by trimming the task #204 anecdote out of the description (it lives in the body's worked-example section anyway). Keep the three core claims. Don't break them up further.

---

### Finding 3 — "deeply nested decision tree" (severity 4) → ⚠️ disagree with diagnosis, but useful suggestion

**Linter's take:** "Sequential phases and their substeps create a deeply nested decision tree, making it difficult for the model to track progress."

**My take:** The diagnosis is wrong. The skill is **linear**, not branching:

```
Phase 1 → land all → Phase 2 (loop until 2 clean rounds) → land all → Phase 3 → land all → verify
```

That's a sequence with one bounded loop, not a decision tree. A "deep decision tree" would be: *"if A and B then X; if A and not B then Y; if not A and C then..."* — none of that exists here. There are no branches in this skill; every path leads to "land everything and continue".

But the linter's *suggestion* — "use a numbered list or flowchart to make sequence explicit" — does have value, just not for the stated reason. A 5-line *quick-flow* at the top would help a model orient before reading the dense body. Not because the structure is wrong, but because a TL;DR is always helpful in a 266-line file.

**Action:** Add a `## Quick flow` block at the top showing the linear sequence. Don't restructure the body — the body is correctly linear already.

---

### Finding 4 — "'next iteration' is ambiguous" (severity 2) → ✅ trivial fix worth landing

**My take:** Hair-splitting, but harmless to fix. "Next iteration" in SWE context means "future sprint/PR/cycle" — unambiguous in practice. But explicit is better than implicit, and the replacement is one word.

**Action:** Land — `"don't promise 'next iteration'"` → `"don't promise a future PR / sprint"`.

---

### Finding 5 — "no guidance when both spec and impl are wrong" (severity 2) → ✅ real gap, worth fixing

**My take:** This is the most useful finding. Phase 1's *"best outcome independent of either side"* framing implicitly covers the both-wrong case ("sometimes it's a totally different thing"), but the skill doesn't tell the model **how to handle that case differently** from routine drift.

In practice, when both sides are wrong, the right move differs materially:
- Routine drift → land the fix unilaterally with "best outcome" justification.
- **Both-wrong case → escalate to user via `AskUserQuestion`** because it's a real design decision, not a routine drift fix. Then land the new design in both spec and impl together, plus tests proving the new design.

The skill currently lumps these together. Splitting them out is real value.

**Action:** Land — add a paragraph to Phase 1 covering this case explicitly.

---

### Finding 6 — "missing error handling for absent spec/impl files" (severity 2) → ⚠️ mostly hypothetical, but one defensive line worth adding

**My take:** Mostly hypothetical because CLAUDE.md step 3 ("Review the cited spec sections") already requires the spec to exist before implementation starts. If the spec is missing, the workflow already broke upstream — this isn't an audit-time problem.

The one real case worth covering: a brand-new component being implemented before its spec exists (a workflow violation, but it happens). The right response there is to **surface it and halt the audit**, not to try to audit against a non-existent spec.

**Action:** Land — add one sentence to Phase 1 saying "if the cited spec is missing, surface that to the user and halt the audit; an unspecced module is a planning failure, not an audit task."

---

## Summary

| # | Worth landing? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Ambiguous trigger phrases | ✅ Yes (small) | Add "any of these" |
| 2. Constraint overload in description | ✅ Yes (partial) | Trim to ~95 words; keep the three claims |
| 3. Deep decision tree | ⚠️ Disagree with diagnosis | But add a Quick-flow TL;DR |
| 4. "Next iteration" ambiguous | ✅ Yes (trivial) | One-word replacement |
| 5. Both-spec-and-impl-wrong gap | ✅ Yes (real) | Add paragraph to Phase 1 |
| 6. Missing-file error handling | ⚠️ Mostly hypothetical | One defensive sentence |

The high-severity findings (1, 2, 3) are real-but-overcalibrated — the linter is right that those areas have *something* to improve, but its suggested fixes are partly generic-template advice that doesn't fit this kind of workflow skill. The medium-severity findings (4, 5, 6) are smaller in claim but actually more accurate — finding 5 in particular is a real gap.

Want me to land the six fixes I marked ✅? They're a total of ~20 lines of changes, no restructuring.

content.text: ### Fix 3: add Quick flow TL;DR

input.old_string:
# Post-implementation audit

This skill is the bridge between *"the named tests pass"* and *"the user reviews the diff"*. It exists because the initial implementation of any task is usually correct on its surface acceptance criteria but quietly drifts from the spec, leaves untested defensive code, ships APIs that diverge from convention, or carries a fold-vs-lowercase mistake that won't surface until production. Running these audits before user-review collapses three reviewer round-trips into one.

The skill is mandatory per CLAUDE.md "Development Workflow" step 7 — run it after implementing any user-requested task (feature, bug fix, refactor) and before presenting the final result for user review.
input.new_string:
# Post-implementation audit

This skill is the bridge between *"the named tests pass"* and *"the user reviews the diff"*. It exists because the initial implementation of any task is usually correct on its surface acceptance criteria but quietly drifts from the spec, leaves untested defensive code, ships APIs that diverge from convention, or carries a fold-vs-lowercase mistake that won't surface until production. Running these audits before user-review collapses three reviewer round-trips into one.

The skill is mandatory per CLAUDE.md "Development Workflow" step 7 — run it after implementing any user-requested task (feature, bug fix, refactor) and before presenting the final result for user review.

## Quick flow

The audit is strictly linear — no branching, just a loop in Phase 2:

```
Phase 1 (spec/impl drift audit)
   └─→ land all Phase 1 findings
        └─→ Phase 2 (iterative "anything missing?")
              ├─→ Round 1: surface findings → land all
              ├─→ Round 2: surface findings → land all
              └─→ keep looping until 2 consecutive clean rounds (minimum 2 rounds total)
                   └─→ Phase 3 (test coverage)
                        └─→ land all Phase 3 findings
                             └─→ Final verification suite
                                  └─→ Hand off to user (CLAUDE.md step 8)

# Post-implementation audit

This skill is the bridge between *"the named tests pass"* and *"the user reviews the diff"*. It exists because the initial implementation of any task is usually correct on its surface acceptance criteria but quietly drifts from the spec, leaves untested defensive code, ships APIs that diverge from convention, or carries a fold-vs-lowercase mistake that won't surface until production. Running these audits before user-review collapses three reviewer round-trips into one.

The skill is mandatory per CLAUDE.md "Development Workflow" step 7 — run it after implementing any user-requested task (feature, bug fix, refactor) and before presenting the final result for user review.
toolUseResult.newString:
# Post-implementation audit

This skill is the bridge between *"the named tests pass"* and *"the user reviews the diff"*. It exists because the initial implementation of any task is usually correct on its surface acceptance criteria but quietly drifts from the spec, leaves untested defensive code, ships APIs that diverge from convention, or carries a fold-vs-lowercase mistake that won't surface until production. Running these audits before user-review collapses three reviewer round-trips into one.

The skill is mandatory per CLAUDE.md "Development Workflow" step 7 — run it after implementing any user-requested task (feature, bug fix, refactor) and before presenting the final result for user review.

## Quick flow

The audit is strictly linear — no branching, just a loop in Phase 2:

```
Phase 1 (spec/impl drift audit)
   └─→ land all Phase 1 findings
        └─→ Phase 2 (iterative "anything missing?")
              ├─→ Round 1: surface findings → land all
              ├─→ Round 2: surface findings → land all
              └─→ keep looping until 2 consecutive clean rounds (minimum 2 rounds total)
                   └─→ Phase 3 (test coverage)
                        └─→ land all Phase 3 findings
                             └─→ Final verification suite
                                  └─→ Hand off to user (CLAUDE.md step 8)
```

The body below walks through each phase in order. Read top-to-bottom; the structure mirrors the execution order.

lines: The skill is mandatory per CLAUDE.md "Development Workflow" step 7 — run it after implementing any user-requested task (feature, bug fix, refactor) and before presenting the final result for user review.
+The audit is strictly linear — no branching, just a loop in Phase 2:
+              └─→ keep looping until 2 consecutive clean rounds (minimum 2 rounds total)
+The body below walks through each phase in order. Read top-to-bottom; the structure mirrors the execution order.

**Every recommendation each audit phase produces lands in *this* PR.** Don't open follow-up issues. Don't TODO-flag. Don't promise "next iteration". Don't quietly skip a finding because it feels like scope creep.

5. Group findings by severity (High / Medium / Low) and present as a table to the user.

input.new_string:
5. Group findings by severity (High / Medium / Low) and present as a table to the user.
6. Land all of them. Update the spec where the impl is right, update the impl where the spec is right, do both where neither was right, change *only* the spec wording (and add tests proving the new wording) when the contract was subtly wrong all along.

#### Special case: both the spec and the implementation are wrong

A small fraction of drift findings are *not* "one side is right, the other drifted" — both sides are incorrect, incomplete, or built on a flawed premise. This is rare but real (the task #204 eszett case is an example: the spec implied `ß ↔ ss` equivalence, the impl used `to_lowercase`, but neither matched the actual TR21 case-folding semantics needed). Handle this case differently from routine drift:

1. **Escalate to the user via `AskUserQuestion`** — don't unilaterally pick a "best outcome" when no precedent exists. A both-wrong case is a real design decision, not a routine drift fix; the user owns the call.
2. **Once the user picks a direction**, land *all three* together in this same PR: the new spec wording, the new implementation, and the tests proving the new design. Skipping any of the three leaves a different kind of drift behind.
3. **Write the test first** (TDD per CLAUDE.md), watch it fail under the old code, then change the impl, then update the spec to describe what the new tests assert.

content.text:
All six fixes landed and verified.

## Summary of changes

| Fix | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Trigger phrases ambiguity** | *"Also trigger on phrases like X, Y, Z"* | *"Invoke … OR when any of these phrases appears: X, Y, Z"* |
| **2. Description length / constraint overload** | 124 words | **85 words** (under the ~100 target; removed the task #204 anecdote — it still lives in the body's worked-example section) |
| **3. Decision-tree concern** | Body started directly with operating principle + phases | Added new **`## Quick flow`** section at the top (lines 12–29) showing the strictly linear sequence with the one bounded loop in Phase 2 |
| **4. "Next iteration" ambiguous** | *"Don't promise 'next iteration'"* | *"Don't promise a future PR or a future sprint"* |
| **5. Both-spec-and-impl-wrong gap** | Implicit in *"best outcome ... sometimes a totally different thing"* | New **`#### Special case: both the spec and the implementation are wrong`** section in Phase 1 — escalate via `AskUserQuestion`, land all three (spec + impl + tests) together, TDD ordering. References the task #204 eszett case as the canonical example. |
| **6. Missing-spec error handling** | No guidance | New **`#### Precondition: both files must exist`** subsection at the top of Phase 1 — stop the audit and surface to user if spec/impl files are missing or empty. |

Skill is now **297 lines** (was 266; +31 lines for the 6 fixes) — still comfortably under the 500-line target. The description is at 85 words, comfortably under the framework's ~100 recommendation. Quick-flow gives a model 5-line orientation before the body, so the linear sequence is visible without needing to read the whole file.

The two findings I pushed back on (constraint overload, deep decision tree) got *partial* fixes that addressed the kernel of truth in each — the description shortened, the linear structure made explicit — without restructuring the body in ways that would have lost real content.

1	# ars-ui
2	
3	## Project Overview
4	
5	Rust frontend component library using state machines, framework-agnostic core with Leptos/Dioxus adapters.
6	
7	## Current Phase
8	
9	The repo is now in active implementation, not spec drafting only.
10	
11	Agents working on implementation should use the GitHub Project roadmap and issue backlog as the execution source of truth:
12	
13	- Use the GitHub Project `ars-ui implementation roadmap` to understand active epics, task breakdown, dependencies, status, and iteration planning.
14	- Prefer picking a single issue-backed task that is unblocked, sized, and scoped for independent delivery.
15	- Do not start work from an epic issue unless the user explicitly asks for planning or further decomposition.
16	- Do not start a task that is blocked by unresolved GitHub issue dependencies.
17	- Treat native GitHub issue dependencies as the blocker graph and the issue body acceptance criteria as the delivery contract.
18	
19	## Development Workflow
20	
21	For implementation tasks:
22	
23	1. Read the assigned or selected GitHub issue first.
24	2. Move the issue to **In Progress** on the GitHub Project board.
25	3. Review the cited spec sections and any dependency issues.
26	4. Add or update the named tests first.
27	5. Implement only the scope required to make those tests pass.
28	6. If implementation changes the intended contract, update the relevant spec in the same task.
29	7. **MANDATORY:** Invoke the `post-implementation-audit` skill (`.claude/skills/post-implementation-audit/SKILL.md`). It runs three sequential audits on the new code — (a) spec/implementation drift with "best outcome" recommendations, (b) iterative "anything else missing?" passes (minimum two rounds), (c) test-coverage audit across every test type the workspace uses (unit, snapshot, proptest, mutation, doc, spec-conformance, llvm-cov). **Every finding lands in _this_ PR — no deferral, no follow-up issues.** This step exists because initial implementations consistently ship spec drift and silent contract violations that take 3+ review round-trips to surface; the audit collapses them into one. Skipping this step is a contract violation.
30	8. Present the final result for user review before any commit.
31	9. After user approval, run `cargo xci` locally and fix any failures before pushing anything to GitHub. During normal implementation, reserve `cargo xci` for substantial chunks of code or the final pre-PR gate; for small follow-up changes, run the focused tests and checks that cover the edited code instead.
32	10. After `cargo xci` passes, commit and push a PR targeting `main`. The PR body MUST include an auto-close keyword for the issue being delivered, for example `Closes #20` or `Fixes #20`, so GitHub closes the issue automatically when the PR merges.
33	11. **If the PR creates or modifies any `.snap` insta fixtures, attach the `snapshot-reviewed` label after opening or updating it** (`gh pr edit <num> --add-label "snapshot-reviewed"`). This signals to reviewers that the snapshot output was inspected and is intentional. Re-apply the label whenever you push a commit that touches `.snap` files; the workflow assumes the label reflects the latest snapshot state.
34	12. Only after CI passes and the PR is merged, close the issue.
35	
36	Never close a GitHub issue without a merged PR that passes CI. Never commit or push without explicit user approval. Keep the issue, PR, and Project board status aligned with the actual work state at every step.
37	
38	Default delivery rules:
39	
40	- Follow TDD: tests first, implementation second, verification last.
41	- Keep task scope aligned with the issue. If the issue is too large or ambiguous, stop and split or clarify rather than freelancing a bigger change.
42	- Prefer tasks sized `1`, `2`, `3`, or `5` points. `8` is exceptional. `13` must be decomposed before implementation.
43	- Preserve the crate and dependency layering defined by the architecture and implementation plan.
44	- Do not add a new dependency crate without explicit user approval first. If a task appears to need a new crate, stop, explain why, and get approval before editing any `Cargo.toml`.
45	- When a task is complete, verify the exact tests and checks named by the issue before considering it done.
46	
47	### Code Quality Standards
48	
49	- **Document all public items.** Every public struct, enum, trait, function, constant, type alias, method, field, and variant must have a `///` doc comment. Every `lib.rs` must have a `//!` crate-level doc comment. The workspace enforces `missing_docs` linting — undocumented public items are build failures.
50	- **Zero warnings policy.** All code must compile with zero warnings under the workspace's configured clippy and rustc lints. Fix the root cause instead of suppressing. When suppression is genuinely needed, use `#[expect(lint, reason = "...")]` — never `#[allow(...)]`.
51	- **Derive documentation from the spec.** Doc comments should describe the _purpose and semantics_ of the item as defined in the corresponding `spec/` files, not just restate the type signature.
52	- **Use `#[inline]` selectively, not mechanically.** Do **not** add Clippy's `missing_inline_in_public_items` lint at the workspace level, and do not treat public visibility alone as a reason to mark an item `#[inline]`. Use `#[inline]` for thin cross-crate wrappers, trivial accessors, and hot-path no-op shims where the call overhead is plausibly meaningful. Avoid blanket `#[inline]` on all public APIs — it increases code size, adds compile-time cost, and turns `#[inline]` into noise instead of a deliberate performance signal.
53	- **Use directory-backed modules with `mod.rs` when a module owns children.** This repo standardizes on the older filesystem layout for nested modules. If module `foo` has child modules, the parent must live at `foo/mod.rs`, not `foo.rs`. Do not mix `foo.rs` with a sibling `foo/` directory. When a refactor adds child modules under an existing flat file, move the parent into `foo/mod.rs` in the same change. Do not leave empty leftover module directories behind.
54	
55	### API Design Standards
56	
57	- **Design for the pit of success.** Public APIs should make the most natural, shortest path also be the path that is correct, accessible, localizable, type-safe, and performant. Prefer ergonomic constructors and defaults that preserve the spec's strongest guarantees instead of making users opt into correctness after choosing a convenient API.
58	- **Name escape hatches explicitly.** When a component needs a lower-level, static, non-i18n, or otherwise less complete path, keep it available but make the tradeoff visible in the API name or type. The blessed path should remain the simplest call site, while escape hatches should read as deliberate choices.
59	- **Keep semantic data separate from rendered views.** Rich framework views are useful for customization, but components still need semantic sources for accessible names, localized text, announcements, ids, and state-machine wiring. Do not rely on arbitrary rendered view trees as the only source of user-facing semantics.
60	
61	### Code Coverage
62	
63	The workspace uses `cargo-llvm-cov` for code coverage with per-crate threshold enforcement. CI runs coverage on every PR (see `.github/workflows/ci.yml`).
64	
65	**Local coverage commands:**
66	
67	```bash
68	# Per-crate coverage with inline annotated source (most useful during development)
69	cargo llvm-cov test -p ars-interactions --text -- hover
70	
71	# Per-crate summary only
72	cargo llvm-cov test -p ars-interactions -- hover
73	
74	# Native-only workspace coverage (host target; excludes wasm-only crates)
75	cargo llvm-cov --workspace \
76	  --exclude ars-leptos --exclude ars-dioxus \
77	  --exclude ars-test-harness-leptos --exclude ars-test-harness-dioxus \
78	  --exclude ars-derive --exclude xtask \
79	  --lcov --output-path lcov.info
80	
81	# Check all crates against spec-defined thresholds (run after a *merged* lcov)
82	cargo xtask coverage check-all --file lcov.info
83	```

1	//! `Highlight` component — text substring matching with Unicode-aware case folding.
2	//!
3	//! `Highlight` is a stateless utility that splits a text into alternating
4	//! highlighted / non-highlighted chunks based on one or more search queries.
5	//! It supports three match strategies (`Contains`, `StartsWith`, `Fuzzy`),
6	//! locale-aware Unicode case mapping via ICU4X `CaseMapper` (handles Turkic
7	//! dotted/dotless I, German eszett, Greek final sigma, Lithuanian dotted I,
8	//! and other CLDR-supplied language-tailored mappings), and multi-query
9	//! merging that consolidates both overlapping **and** adjacent ranges so
10	//! adapters never see back-to-back highlighted chunks.
11	//!
12	//! The component has no state machine. Adapters render each returned
13	//! `HighlightChunk` as a `<mark>` (`highlighted == true`) or `<span>`
14	//! (`highlighted == false`). The agnostic-core consists of `Props`,
15	//! `MatchStrategy`, `HighlightChunk`, the `Part` enum, the `Api` attribute
16	//! mapper, and the `highlight_chunks` standalone computation.
17	//!
18	//! This module is gated on `feature = "i18n"`: the locale-aware contract on
19	//! `Props::ignore_case = true` is part of the spec, so the module is only
20	//! compiled when `ars-i18n/icu4x` is enabled.
21	//!
22	//! See `spec/components/utility/highlight.md` for the authoritative contract.
23	
24	use alloc::{borrow::ToOwned as _, string::String, vec, vec::Vec};
25	
26	use ars_core::{AttrMap, ComponentPart, ConnectApi, HtmlAttr};
27	use ars_i18n::Locale;
28	
29	/// Props for the [`Highlight`](crate::utility::highlight) component.
30	///
31	/// `Highlight` does not carry a DOM `id` — chunk output is a sequence of
32	/// adapter-rendered children, not a single identifiable element — so [`Props`]
33	/// does not derive [`HasId`](ars_core::HasId) like other stateless utilities.
34	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
35	pub struct Props {
36	    /// The search queries to highlight. Supports a single query string or
37	    /// multiple queries to highlight simultaneously (e.g., multiple search
38	    /// terms). When multiple queries are provided, all matches from all
39	    /// queries are highlighted; overlapping ranges are merged into a single
40	    /// highlighted chunk. An empty vec, or a vec where every entry is empty,
41	    /// produces a single non-highlighted chunk containing the full text.
42	    pub query: Vec<String>,
43	
44	    /// The full text to search within.
45	    pub text: String,
46	
47	    /// When `true` (default), matching is case-insensitive via Unicode case
48	    /// folding — `ars_i18n::to_lowercase` with the [`Locale`] passed to
49	    /// [`highlight_chunks`] / [`Api::chunks`]. When `false`, byte-exact
50	    /// matching is used (no normalisation pass; the locale parameter is
51	    /// ignored).
52	    pub ignore_case: bool,
53	
54	    /// The strategy used to match the query against the text. Defaults to
55	    /// [`MatchStrategy::Contains`].
56	    pub match_strategy: MatchStrategy,
57	}
58	
59	impl Default for Props {
60	    fn default() -> Self {
61	        Self {
62	            query: Vec::new(),
63	            text: String::new(),
64	            ignore_case: true,
65	            match_strategy: MatchStrategy::Contains,
66	        }
67	    }
68	}
69	
70	impl Props {
71	    /// Returns fresh [`Props`] with the documented defaults — equivalent
72	    /// to [`Default::default`], offered as the entry point for the
73	    /// builder chain.
74	    #[must_use]
75	    pub fn new() -> Self {
76	        Self::default()
77	    }
78	
79	    /// Sets the query list. Accepts any iterable of `Into<String>`, so
80	    /// callers can pass `["foo"]`, `vec!["foo".to_string(), "bar".into()]`,
81	    /// `&["foo", "bar"][..]`, etc.
82	    #[must_use]
83	    pub fn query<I, S>(mut self, query: I) -> Self
84	    where
85	        I: IntoIterator<Item = S>,
86	        S: Into<String>,
87	    {
88	        self.query = query.into_iter().map(Into::into).collect();
89	        self
90	    }
91	
92	    /// Sets the text to search within.
93	    #[must_use]
94	    pub fn text(mut self, text: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
95	        self.text = text.into();
96	        self
97	    }
98	
99	    /// Sets the case-sensitivity flag.
100	    #[must_use]
101	    pub const fn ignore_case(mut self, value: bool) -> Self {
102	        self.ignore_case = value;
103	        self
104	    }
105	
106	    /// Sets the match strategy.
107	    #[must_use]
108	    pub const fn match_strategy(mut self, value: MatchStrategy) -> Self {
109	        self.match_strategy = value;
110	        self
111	    }
112	}
113	
114	/// The strategy used to match the query against the text.
115	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Default, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
116	pub enum MatchStrategy {
117	    /// Highlight every occurrence of the query within the text. The default
118	    /// — the most permissive strategy, matching Ark UI's default.
119	    #[default]
120	    Contains,
121	
122	    /// Highlight only the leading run of the text when it starts with the
123	    /// query (after case folding, if `ignore_case` is `true`).
124	    StartsWith,
125	
126	    /// Subsequence match: highlight each individual query character within
127	    /// the text in order, allowing arbitrary gaps.
128	    ///
129	    /// All query characters must be present (in order, not necessarily
130	    /// contiguous). When the full subsequence is found, each matched
131	    /// character contributes its own one-char highlighted range. When any
132	    /// query character cannot be located, **no fuzzy ranges are emitted
133	    /// for that query** (all-or-nothing) — partial matches would highlight
134	    /// characters that don't represent the query and confuse the user.
135	    Fuzzy,
136	}
137	
138	/// One contiguous run of the original text, tagged as highlighted or not.
139	///
140	/// `text` borrows from [`Props::text`] (the lifetime is tied to the props
141	/// passed to [`highlight_chunks`] / [`Api::chunks`]); the chunk vector is
142	/// owned by the caller. Adapters render highlighted chunks as `<mark>` and
143	/// non-highlighted chunks as `<span>` per the component anatomy.
144	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
145	pub struct HighlightChunk<'a> {
146	    /// The slice of the source text covered by this chunk.
147	    pub text: &'a str,
148	
149	    /// Whether the adapter should wrap this chunk in `<mark>`.
150	    pub highlighted: bool,
151	}
152	
153	/// DOM parts of the `Highlight` component.
154	///
155	/// Only [`Part::Root`] is a static anatomy slot. The per-chunk
156	/// `<mark>` / `<span>` elements are emitted dynamically per
157	/// [`HighlightChunk`], parametric on the runtime `highlighted` boolean — a
158	/// value that [`ConnectApi::part_attrs`] (which takes a `Part` by value, no
159	/// payload) cannot express. Adapters call [`Api::chunk_attrs`] per chunk
160	/// instead. See `spec/components/utility/highlight.md` §2 for the rendered
161	/// shape.
162	#[derive(ComponentPart)]
163	#[scope = "highlight"]
164	pub enum Part {
165	    /// The root `<span>` that wraps the chunk children.
166	    Root,
167	}
168	
169	/// Attribute-mapper API for the `Highlight` component.
170	///
171	/// Constructed via [`Api::new`] from [`Props`] and queried via
172	/// [`Api::root_attrs`] / [`Api::chunk_attrs`] for adapter-rendered DOM
173	/// attributes, or [`Api::chunks`] / the standalone [`highlight_chunks`]
174	/// function for the chunk sequence itself.
175	#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
176	pub struct Api {
177	    props: Props,
178	}
179	
180	impl Api {
181	    /// Wraps the given props in an `Api`. The props are stored verbatim and
182	    /// can be read back via [`Api::props`] or the typed accessors.
183	    #[must_use]
184	    pub const fn new(props: Props) -> Self {
185	        Self { props }
186	    }
187	
188	    /// Returns a reference to the underlying [`Props`].
189	    #[must_use]
190	    pub const fn props(&self) -> &Props {
191	        &self.props
192	    }
193	
194	    /// Returns the configured query list.
195	    #[must_use]
196	    pub fn query(&self) -> &[String] {
197	        &self.props.query
198	    }

attachment.content:
=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

===================================

content.text:
`cargo xci` is running in the background (process `bhj9zuaf2`). I'll be notified on completion. While I wait, here's the state of play:

**Pre-PR checklist (so far):**
- ✅ Two stale `to_lowercase` doc references in `highlight.rs` fixed
- ✅ AGENTS.md skill path corrected from `.claude/skills/...` (symlink) to canonical `.agents/skills/...`
- ⏳ `cargo xci` running
- 📋 Main is 1 commit ahead (`b018b611 Fix Nightly tabs regressions (#646)`) — will rebase after xci passes
- 📋 26 files to commit (1 new module + 9 snapshots + 1 new spec-conformance test + 1 new skill + 14 modifications)

**Draft commit message** (will use after xci + rebase):

> feat(components): implement Highlight agnostic core (#204)
>
> Closes #204
>
> - Add `utility::highlight` module to `ars-components` (Props, MatchStrategy, HighlightChunk, Part, Api, highlight_chunks) gated on the new `i18n` feature
> - Three match strategies (Contains, StartsWith, Fuzzy) with TR21 Unicode case folding via new `ars_i18n::case_fold` helper (`ß ↔ ss ↔ SS`, Turkic `İ ↔ i`, Greek final-sigma collapse)
> - Multi-query overlapping/adjacent range merging in the core (adapters never see back-to-back highlighted chunks)
> - 41 unit tests + 9 insta snapshots + proptest invariants + spec-conformance test
> - 0 missed mutations (`cargo mutants`), 99.91% line coverage, all clippy/doc/spec gates clean
> - Adapter feature wiring: `ars-leptos/icu4x` and `ars-dioxus/icu4x` now propagate `ars-components/i18n`
> - Spec updates: core, Leptos adapter, Dioxus adapter, foundation/10 template parametric-anatomy section
> - Add `post-implementation-audit` skill + mandatory CLAUDE.md step 7 codifying the audit flow this PR was driven through
>
> Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

I'll proceed with the rebase + commit + push when xci completes.

=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

===================================

content.subject: Move issue #204 to In Progress on GitHub Project board
content.description: Per CLAUDE.md workflow step 2, set status to In Progress for issue #204 on the ars-ui implementation roadmap project.

content.subject: Write Highlight unit + snapshot tests first (TDD)
content.description: Add the full test suite (tests #1–#30 from plan) to crates/ars-components/src/utility/highlight.rs before any implementation logic. Tests must compile but should be expected to fail until the implementation lands.

content.subject: Implement Props, MatchStrategy, HighlightChunk, Part, Api, highlight_chunks
content.description: Write the highlight.rs module per plan: public types, attribute helpers, and the matching pipeline (Contains/StartsWith/Fuzzy + multi-query merge + Unicode case folding via ars_i18n::to_lowercase).

content.subject: Register module + run focused tests and snapshots
content.description: Add pub mod highlight; entry to utility/mod.rs (alphabetised), then run focused unit tests, accept snapshots, and re-run to confirm green.

content.subject: Fix two spec drift items in highlight.md
content.description: Update spec/components/utility/highlight.md: (a) reword line 65 to describe ars_i18n::to_lowercase rather than ICU4X fold methods; (b) fix line 111 example to pass &locale.

content.subject: Run final verification (clippy, doc, llvm-cov, spec-tool validate)
content.description: Run the verification commands from the plan and confirm zero warnings, docs build, coverage acceptable, spec-tool validate clean.

content.subject: Present final result for user review
content.description: Show the diff summary, test results, and any spec edits. Wait for explicit approval before cargo xci / commit / push / PR.

content.subject: Apply audit findings (drift fixes) to impl + spec
content.description: Land all audit recommendations: gate Highlight on i18n (drop fallback), add dir=auto to root, refine derives + docs, rewrite spec sections H1/H2/M1/M3/M4/M5/L1-L6/X1-X3.

content.subject: Run cargo xci, rebase main, commit, push PR
content.description: Final pre-PR gate per CLAUDE.md steps 9-12. Run cargo xci, fix any failures, fetch main + rebase, commit with co-author, push with -u, open PR with Closes #204, add snapshot-reviewed label.
content.activeForm: Running cargo xci and opening PR

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(components): implement Highlight agnostic core

Closes #204

Add the framework-agnostic `Highlight` utility component to
`ars-components::utility::highlight`. Stateless text-substring matcher
with three strategies (Contains / StartsWith / Fuzzy), TR21 Unicode case
folding via the new `ars_i18n::case_fold` helper (so `ß ↔ ss ↔ SS`,
Turkic `İ ↔ i`, Greek final-sigma collapse, and CLDR-supplied tailored
mappings work in both directions), and multi-query merging that
consolidates overlapping and adjacent ranges in the core so adapters
never see back-to-back highlighted chunks.

API surface (matches the stateless-utility template):

- `Props` (Default, builder, query/text/ignore_case/match_strategy)
- `MatchStrategy` (Contains default per Ark UI parity)
- `HighlightChunk<'a>` (Copy, Eq, Hash; borrows from Props.text)
- `Part::Root` + `Api` (root_attrs sets dir="auto"; chunk_attrs(bool)
  for the parametric per-chunk slot; ConnectApi dispatch on Root)
- Standalone `highlight_chunks(&Props, &Locale)` per spec §1.2

Build wiring:

- New `ars-components/i18n = ["ars-i18n/icu4x"]` feature; the
  `utility::highlight` module is `#[cfg(feature = "i18n")]` because the
  locale-aware contract on `ignore_case = true` requires ICU4X.
- `ars-leptos/icu4x` and `ars-dioxus/icu4x` now propagate
  `ars-components/i18n` so adapter builds with icu4x can use Highlight.
- Added `ars_i18n::case_fold` (gated on `icu4x`); wraps
  `CaseMapper::fold_string` / `fold_turkic_string` based on locale.

Tests:

- 41 unit tests covering every strategy, every edge case, full
  ignore_case matrix, ß/SS/Turkic three-direction equivalence, fold
  partial-match defenses, multi-query overlap + full-contain merging
  branches, and Api accessor positive/negative directions
- 9 insta snapshots (root, chunk attrs ×2, chunks for each strategy +
  multi-query + Turkic + German eszett)
- 1 inline doc test with the canonical usage example
- 1 proptest invariant block (roundtrip, no-adjacent-highlighted,
  no-empty-chunks, empty-edge-case) over 8 locales
- 1 spec-conformance test asserting Part enum matches anatomy table
- 4 new ars_i18n::case_fold tests (eszett, sigma, Turkic, idempotence)

Verification: `cargo xci` clean across all 20 steps (fmt, check,
clippy, unit, i18n-browser, dom-browser, release, integration,
adapter, adapter-parity, coverage, snapshot-count,
spec-compile-snippets, error-variant-coverage, 5 feature matrices,
mutual-exclusion). `cargo mutants -p ars-components -f
crates/ars-components/src/utility/highlight.rs` reports 57/57 caught,
0 missed. Line coverage 99.91% (1 line is a defensive
zero-length-highlight guard already documented as an equivalent
mutation in `.cargo/mutants.toml`).

Spec updates (same PR — no deferral):

- `spec/components/utility/highlight.md`: complete rewrite of §1.2
  (Connect / API with Part + Api alongside the standalone function),
  §2 anatomy table annotates Chunk as a parametric slot, §3.1 moves
  adjacency consolidation from "adapters SHOULD" to "core does it",
  §3.2 says core emits dir="auto", §4 adapter example uses
  Api::root_attrs/chunk_attrs spread, §1.1 adds Default/Eq/Hash
  derives and the builder pattern, §1.3 documents the i18n feature
  gate, X1/X2 add Eq/Copy/Hash to MatchStrategy and HighlightChunk,
  X3 softens the unsourced screen-reader claim.
- `spec/leptos-components/utility/highlight.md` and
  `spec/dioxus-components/utility/highlight.md`: §5 attr-merge tables
  now describe Api::root_attrs/chunk_attrs and dir="auto" as
  core-owned, §23 framework-specific behaviour aligned to the new
  core, §24 canonical implementation sketches use Api spread and
  pass &locale, §27 a11y notes reflect core-side consolidation.
- `spec/foundation/10-component-spec-template.md`: new §4.2
  subsection documenting the "static vs parametric anatomy slot"
  convention (the Highlight chunk being the canonical example).

Codify the audit flow:

- New `.agents/skills/post-implementation-audit/SKILL.md` (297 lines):
  three sequential phases — (a) spec/impl drift audit with
  "best outcome" recommendations that disregard either side as
  default, (b) iterative "anything else missing?" passes until two
  consecutive clean rounds, (c) test-coverage audit across every
  test type (unit, snapshot, proptest, mutation, doc,
  spec-conformance, llvm-cov + xtask wasm). Every finding lands in
  the same PR — no deferral. Includes the both-spec-and-impl-wrong
  escalation case (the eszett finding on this very PR), missing-file
  precondition, anti-patterns, and the task #204 worked example.
- `AGENTS.md` step 7: new MANDATORY step invoking the
  post-implementation-audit skill before user review.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10
echo "--- commit result ---"
git log --oneline -1 HEAD

content.content:
# Spec Directory Guide

## File Conventions

- `_category.md` files contain category overviews and shared conventions
- Component files use kebab-case matching the component name
- Shared types in `shared/` are referenced by component frontmatter
- Headings are numbered (`## 1. State Machine`, `### 1.1 States`)

## Spec Changes During Implementation

The repo is in active implementation. Spec edits inside `spec/` should usually be tied to a concrete GitHub implementation task or PR, not made as detached cleanup.

- Read the implementation issue before changing spec text.
- Keep cited spec sections synchronized with the implementation task that discovered the mismatch.
- If an adapter-specific implementation exposes a missing shared abstraction, promote that abstraction into the appropriate foundation/shared spec instead of documenting it only in framework code.
- Do not leave placeholder notes for later reconciliation; resolve the spec/code mismatch in the same task whenever possible.

## Adding a New Component

1. Create `components/{category}/{component-name}.md` with YAML frontmatter
2. Follow the section structure defined in `foundation/10-component-spec-template.md` for the appropriate tier
3. Add an entry to `manifest.toml` with path, category, deps, and related
4. Update `foundation/02-component-catalog.md` with the new component
5. Run `cargo run -p spec-tool -- validate` to verify consistency

## Component File Format

```markdown
---
component: ComponentName
category: { category }
tier: stateless | stateful | complex
foundation_deps: [architecture, accessibility, ...]
shared_deps: [date-time-types, ...]
related: [sibling-component, ...]
---

# ComponentName
```

See `foundation/10-component-spec-template.md` for the canonical section structure, ordering rules, conformance checklist, and skeleton examples for each tier.

content.text: Branch pushed. Now open the PR.

input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat(components): implement Highlight agnostic core" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

- Implement framework-agnostic `Highlight` utility (`ars-components::utility::highlight`) per `spec/components/utility/highlight.md` — three match strategies (Contains/StartsWith/Fuzzy), TR21 Unicode case folding, multi-query overlap+adjacency merging, parametric chunk anatomy. Gated on a new `i18n` feature wired through the adapter `icu4x` features so Leptos / Dioxus builds with icu4x pick up Highlight automatically.
- Surface a new `ars_i18n::case_fold` helper that wraps `CaseMapper::fold_string` / `fold_turkic_string`. Switching from `to_lowercase` closes the spec's eszett contract — `"ss"` now genuinely matches `"ß"` in both directions (the original implementation passed its named tests but quietly violated this).
- Codify the audit flow that drove this PR: new `post-implementation-audit` skill at `.agents/skills/post-implementation-audit/SKILL.md`, registered as mandatory step 7 of the AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md development workflow. Three sequential phases (spec/impl drift, iterative "anything missing?", test coverage) that land every finding in the same PR. The skill's worked example references this PR.

Closes #204.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo xci` — all 20 steps pass (fmt, check, clippy, unit, i18n-browser, dom-browser, release, integration, adapter, adapter-parity, coverage, snapshot-count, spec-compile-snippets, error-variant-coverage, 5 feature matrices, mutual-exclusion)
- [x] `cargo test -p ars-components --features i18n --all-targets` — 1502 lib + 4 conformance + 1 doc tests pass
- [x] `cargo test -p ars-components --lib` (no `i18n`) — 1463 pass; Highlight module gated out cleanly
- [x] `cargo test -p ars-i18n --features icu4x case::` — 10 pass (incl. 4 new `case_fold` tests covering eszett expansion, Greek final sigma collapse, Turkic vs non-Turkic, idempotence)
- [x] `cargo mutants -p ars-components -f crates/ars-components/src/utility/highlight.rs` — **57/57 caught, 0 missed** (3 equivalent mutations documented with rationale in `.cargo/mutants.toml`)
- [x] `cargo llvm-cov` — **99.91% line / 100% function / 100% region** for `utility/highlight.rs`
- [x] `cargo xtask spec validate` — 340 files clean
- [x] `cargo build -p ars-leptos --features icu4x` and `cargo build -p ars-dioxus --features icu4x,web` — Highlight reachable in adapter builds
- [x] Spec-conformance test asserts the `Part` enum matches the spec's §2 anatomy table

## What landed

| Area | Files |
|---|---|
| Agnostic core | `crates/ars-components/src/utility/highlight.rs` (new), `crates/ars-components/src/utility/mod.rs`, `crates/ars-components/Cargo.toml` |
| Locale-aware case folding helper | `crates/ars-i18n/src/case.rs`, `crates/ars-i18n/src/lib.rs` |
| Adapter feature wiring | `crates/ars-leptos/Cargo.toml`, `crates/ars-dioxus/Cargo.toml` |
| Tests | 9 insta snapshots + inline doc test + 1 spec-conformance test + 1 proptest invariant block + 41 unit tests |
| Spec | Core spec rewrite, Leptos/Dioxus adapter spec updates, foundation/10 new §4.2 parametric-anatomy convention |
| Workflow codification | `.agents/skills/post-implementation-audit/SKILL.md`, `AGENTS.md` step 7 mandatory invocation, `.cargo/mutants.toml` skip justifications |

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
